data.title: International Paper upgraded at Wells Fargo on plan to split into two companies
data.description:
International Paper (IP) +2.5% in Friday's trading as Wells Fargo upgraded shares to Equal Weight from Underweight with a $40 price target, raised from $36, endorsing the company's plan to spin off its Europe, Middle East and Africa segment as an exit from a difficult-to-implement strategy.

International Paper (IP) has made step-change improvement to its North American operations over the past two years, although plenty of heavy lifting remains to reach lofty 2027 targets, while the EMEA business is behind on its progression, Wells Fargo analyst Gabe Hajde said, and now agrees with management that the distinct regions would perform better as two independent companies and sees a more balanced risk/reward profile at the current share price.

North America-heavy International Paper (IP) recently acquired Europe-heavy DS Smith, and although the company noted each region has been strengthened by the combination, separating them still resembles a decision to reverse the acquisition, as well as the exit of a strategy to be a transatlantic operator, Hajde wrote.

The company guided FY 2026 EBITDA to a range of $3.5B-$3.7B, in line with the $3.64B analyst consensus estimate, with free cash flow forecast at $300M-$500M and capital spending of $1.95B-$2.05B; EBITDA guidance by segment was $2.5B-$2.6B for North America Packaging and $1B-$1.1B for EMEA Packaging.

data.title: Warsh nomination is a ‘pro-market move’ – DCLA’s Sarat Sethi
data.description:
Kevin Warsh’s nomination to Fed chair represents a “pro-market move” that should ultimately benefit investors, according to Sarat Sethi, portfolio manager and equity analyst at DCLA.

Despite initial market volatility following the announcement, Sethi told CNBC that the selection is “net-net, a positive for the market.”

Sethi acknowledged that Warsh enters with a reputation for being hawkish, which creates an interesting dynamic given President Trump’s policy preference, “but given what president Trump is asking for, it’ll be a fine line here,” he said.

The market is currently in an “ask questions period,” with investors selling areas they perceive as most vulnerable until more clarity emerges.

A key benefit of the nomination, according to Sethi, is the expected continuation of deregulation in the financial sector.

He noted that the new Fed chair will have the “ear of the president,” and banks will likely gain greater access to Treasuries, which could help put a lid on interest rates. This trend in financials was strong throughout the previous year before moderating due to credit card concerns and other factors.

Sethi emphasized that the administration’s focus will be on interest rates and housing affordability. “I think that’s what this administration wants to do, wants to free up capital, wants to make things more affordable,” he said. The expectation is for a more free-market approach that allows interest rates to move naturally as policy actions take effect.

The analyst also praised Warsh’s communication abilities, calling him “very articulate” and “very smart,” qualities that the market appreciates. The nomination could also help address concerns about dollar debasement, which has been a fundamental driver behind recent momentum in metals and other assets.

While current market conditions show some volatility, Sethi sees long-term opportunities emerging, particularly in the software sector (IGPT), (XSW).

He pointed to companies like Workday (WDAY) and Salesforce (CRM) trading at 13-14x cash flow—well below their historical multiples of 22-22x—as “pockets of opportunity” for patient investors.

More on the Federal Reserve

  • Trump names Kevin Warsh as pick for Federal Reserve chair
  • Société Générale’s 2016 Fed chair call comes true a decade later as Warsh is nominated
  • Former Federal Reserve Vice Chairman Ferguson: The Fed will stand pat for the next few meetings
  • Why 2026 Could Redefine the U.S. Federal Reserve
  • SA analysts share their thoughts on Trump's selection of Kevin Warsh

data.title: Ameris Bancorp outlines mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth targets for 2026 amid record profitability
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ameris Bancorp (ABCB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO H. Proctor reported "record earnings for 2025 at over $412 million for the year with our diluted EPS hitting $6 per share for the first time in our history," attributing the 15% year-over-year EPS increase to organic growth. He highlighted expanding margins, improving efficiency ratio, and tangible book value growth of over 14%.
  • Proctor emphasized strong capital positions, noting "we paid off all of our sub debt during 2025," and described the share repurchase activity: "we were active in the fourth quarter, buying back almost 1% of our stock at an average price of $72. For the year, we repurchased $77 million or 2% of the company at an average price under $67."
  • Asset generation was described as robust, with earning assets growing "almost 6%" and loans growing "almost 5% in the fourth quarter, even with the elevated CRE payoffs of over $500 million." Loan production reached $2.4 billion for the quarter.
  • On asset quality, Proctor stated "our allowance remains healthy at 1.62% of loans," with CRE and construction concentrations "consistent at 262% and 43%, respectively."
  • Nicole Stokes, CFO, detailed the quarter's results: "we reported net income of $108.4 million or $1.59 per diluted share in the fourth quarter. Our return on assets was 1.57%. Our PPNR ROA was 2.38%, and our return on tangible common equity was 14.5% for the quarter."

Outlook

  • Management stated, "we continue to anticipate loan and deposit growth going forward in that mid-single-digit range and expect that longer-term deposit growth will be the governor of loan growth."
  • Stokes indicated, "I do anticipate the efficiency ratio to return above 50% in the first quarter, especially when you consider our seasonally heavy first quarter payroll taxes and 401(k) contributions."
  • On margin, Stokes commented, "we anticipate we could see some slight margin compression over the next few quarters due to the pressure on deposit costs. As we see loan growth increasing, we believe there will be additional deposit pressure as we fund that growth in '26."
  • Projected net charge-offs are expected "in the 20 to 25 basis point range in 2026."

Financial Results

  • Net interest income increased $7.3 million in the quarter, while net interest margin expanded 5 basis points to 3.85% for the fourth quarter.
  • Tangible book value increased by $1.28 during the fourth quarter to $44.18.
  • Noninterest income decreased $10.5 million this quarter, mostly from seasonal declines in mortgage, while noninterest expense decreased $11.5 million, primarily due to lower compensation and marketing costs.
  • Total assets ended the quarter at $27.5 billion, up from $27.1 billion last quarter.
  • Deposits increased $148 million, with noninterest-bearing deposits representing 28.7% of total deposits at year-end. Brokered deposits were stable at 5% of total deposits.

Q&A

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Questioned loan growth visibility after elevated CRE payoffs and hiring strategy. Proctor responded that payoffs are expected to "moderate as we move into the first quarter and second quarter of the year" and confirmed a selective hiring approach, saying "we hired 21 lenders this year. But net-net, we were up 3."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Asked about margin guidance and expense outlook. Stokes replied, "I feel like 5 to 10 basis points over the next few quarters" for margin compression and estimated first quarter expenses at "somewhere between $154 million, $155 million."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Inquired about new loan production yields and capital deployment. Stokes reported "loan production...right at about 6.35% for the quarter" and deposit production at "right around 2%." Proctor said capital priorities remain "growing into it organically and levering it up," with continued opportunistic share repurchases.
  • John McDonald, Truist: Queried deposit trends. Stokes noted that declines in noninterest-bearing deposits were seasonal and already rebounding.
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Focused on mortgage segment fee income and servicing portfolio. Stokes disclosed the unpaid principal balance was "about $8.7 billion, which is about 4% of Tier 1 capital."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about production increases and competitive landscape. Proctor attributed growth to both banker productivity and market disruption, noting competition is "mainly...on pricing."
  • Christopher Marinac, Janney Montgomery Scott: Explored deposit and treasury management growth strategies. Stokes and Proctor discussed focusing on both growing account numbers and deepening relationships, with a continued emphasis on consumer and commercial deposit growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive to slightly optimistic tone, probing growth sustainability, capital deployment, and margin outlook, with questions frequently focused on forward growth drivers and competitive positioning.
  • Management displayed sustained confidence, highlighting organic growth, capital strength, and a selective approach to hiring. Phrases such as "we feel pretty bullish" and "we were very fortunate with the retention" reflected a stable, positive outlook. In Q&A, management remained consistent in tone, offering detailed explanations and reaffirming guidance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management expressed similar optimism, though the current call included more direct discussion of margin compression and expense normalization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth continued from the previous quarter, with more explicit commentary on margin compression and expense increases in Q1 2026.
  • Capital levels remained robust, with further reduction in sub debt and increased share repurchases in Q4.
  • Loan production increased quarter-over-quarter, reaching $2.4 billion, with asset growth and tangible book value also rising.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted slightly from competitive landscape and loan production to expense management, deposit mix, and margin sustainability.
  • Management maintained a positive and confident tone in both quarters, but the current quarter’s commentary included more caution around margin dynamics and deposit costs.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited potential for margin compression due to deposit cost pressures as loan growth accelerates.
  • Expense increases are anticipated in the first quarter, primarily from payroll taxes and incentive accruals.
  • Elevated CRE payoffs in the fourth quarter were described as unusual but expected to moderate.
  • Competition, particularly on pricing for loans and deposits, was acknowledged, with management emphasizing focus on core deposit relationships over rate-driven deposit gathering.

Final Takeaway

Ameris Bancorp management emphasized record organic profitability in 2025, with robust capital returns and strong asset generation positioning the company for continued growth in 2026. The bank projects mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth, anticipates some margin compression as deposit costs rise, and remains committed to disciplined expense management and selective hiring. The outlook is supported by stable asset quality, strong capital levels, and a focus on organic franchise expansion in high-growth Southeastern markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Weyerhaeuser targets $1.5B incremental EBITDA by 2030 while advancing timberlands optimization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Weyerhaeuser Company (WY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Devin Stockfish stated that for the full year, Weyerhaeuser reported GAAP earnings of $324 million or $0.45 per diluted share on net sales of $6.9 billion, and fourth quarter GAAP earnings of $74 million or $0.10 per diluted share on net sales of $1.5 billion. Excluding special items, the fourth quarter saw a loss of $67 million or ($0.09) per diluted share, with adjusted EBITDA of $140 million.
  • Stockfish highlighted the completion of two divestiture transactions in the fourth quarter covering non-core timberlands in Oregon, Georgia, and Alabama for total proceeds of $406 million and noted an agreement to divest 108,000 acres in Virginia for $193 million.
  • The company increased its base dividend by 5% and returned $766 million of cash to shareholders, including $160 million of share repurchase.
  • Stockfish announced, "We exceeded our multiyear target to reach $100 million of annual adjusted EBITDA by year-end 2025" and set a new target to grow the Climate Solutions business to $250 million of annual EBITDA by 2030.
  • CFO David Wold stated, "We generated $562 million of cash from operations. Excluding a $200 million contribution related to pension liability management, cash from operations would be $762 million for the year."
  • Wold added, "We completed our prior $1 billion share repurchase program and announced a new $1 billion authorization in 2025."

Outlook

  • Wold reported that for timberlands, "we expect first quarter earnings before special items and adjusted EBITDA to be comparable to the fourth quarter of 2025."
  • For 2026, Weyerhaeuser expects total company-wide fee harvest volumes of approximately 35.5 million tons, with the South slightly higher than last year, the West comparable, and the North slightly lower.
  • Strategic Land Solutions, the new name for Real Estate, Energy and Natural Resources, expects full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA of approximately $425 million. First quarter earnings for the segment are expected to be approximately $75 million higher than the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Capital expenditures in 2026 are projected at $400 million to $450 million, excluding a $300 million investment for the new EWP facility in Arkansas.
  • Stockfish reiterated, "We intend to catalyze growth initiatives across the entirety of our integrated platform to significantly grow the value and cash generation capabilities of our company and further strengthen our competitive position."

Financial Results

  • Timberlands contributed $50 million to fourth quarter earnings, with adjusted EBITDA of $114 million, down $34 million from the third quarter, mainly due to lower sales volumes and realizations in the West.
  • Southern Timberlands adjusted EBITDA was $69 million, a $5 million decrease sequentially, while Real Estate and ENR delivered $84 million to earnings with adjusted EBITDA of $95 million, slightly above the previous quarter.
  • The average price for real estate sales reached a record high in the fourth quarter at over $8,200 per acre, driven by high-value development transactions in South Carolina.
  • Climate Solutions delivered $119 million in full year adjusted EBITDA, marking a 42% increase over 2024, and the company surpassed the $100 million target for the segment.
  • Wood Products recorded a $78 million loss in Q4, with adjusted EBITDA at a $20 million loss amid "extremely challenging lumber and OSB markets."

Q&A

  • Hamir Patel, CIBC: Asked if improvements in lumber and OSB pricing were due to curtailments or demand. Stockfish replied, "It's primarily driven by curtailment activity...as we continue to approach the spring building season, you do typically start to see some level of pickup in demand."
  • Charles Perron-Piché, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about inventory levels and timberland A&D decisions. Stockfish noted, "Inventories across the channel are in a pretty reasonable state for this time of year...optimistic that we could see some nice pickup in demand as we hit the seasonal spring building season." Wold added, "We continue to see a very solid market right now" for timberland transactions.
  • Anthony Pettinari, Citigroup: Questioned operating rates and steps to improve profitability. Stockfish explained, "In Q4, in lumber, we were sort of in that mid-70% operating rate...over time, it all comes down to where are you on the cost curve relative to the rest of the industry."
  • Ketan Mamtora, BMO: Asked about leverage. Wold answered, "We have tracked higher from a leverage perspective. But as you know, again, that 3.5x net leverage target that we have is a mid-cycle number."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly cautious tone, probing on pricing drivers, capital allocation, and market outlooks. Their inquiries focused on management's flexibility and resilience amid challenging product markets.
  • Management's tone was largely confident, emphasizing execution of strategic initiatives and optimism for long-term growth, with Stockfish stating, "I'm very confident in our ability to achieve our 2030 growth plan."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management conveyed a more constructive view of the longer-term outlook, but acknowledged persistent volatility in the near term.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reiterated completion of portfolio optimization and set more ambitious growth targets for Climate Solutions and company-wide EBITDA.
  • Guidance language shifted from caution about market headwinds in Q3 to a more assertive posture on growth initiatives and capital returns.
  • Analysts continued to question near-term headwinds in wood products, leverage, and capital allocation, but focused more on execution toward new targets than in Q3.
  • Key metric changes included a lower adjusted EBITDA in timberlands and wood products, but higher real estate and Climate Solutions contributions.
  • Management's sentiment was more forward-looking, with increased emphasis on executing a multi-year growth plan.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "extremely challenging market conditions" in wood products and persistent headwinds in end markets.
  • Exposure to soft lumber and OSB pricing, and the impact of elevated log inventories at mills, were noted.
  • Capital allocation discipline and maintenance of investment-grade credit rating remain priorities amid elevated leverage.
  • Regulatory changes in Western Washington are being monitored, but management does not anticipate a meaningful impact.

Final Takeaway

Weyerhaeuser management underscored resilience in a difficult market year, exceeding multiyear targets and launching a strategic plan to deliver $1.5 billion in incremental adjusted EBITDA by 2030. The company optimized its timberlands portfolio, increased its base dividend, and set new growth objectives for its Climate Solutions business, while maintaining a strong balance sheet and demonstrating flexibility in capital allocation to drive long-term shareholder value.

data.title: St. Louis Fed's Musalem says lowering rates now would be 'unadvisable'
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem is in the camp that the U.S. central bank should hold interest rates steady at this time, "with inflation above target and the risks to the outlook evenly balanced."

I believe it would be unadvisable to lower the rate into accommodative territory at this time,” Musalem said Friday in a speech prepared for delivery at an event in Rogers, Arkansas. The Fed held rates steady earlier in the week following three consecutive reductions late last year.

“Aside from risking higher or more persistent inflation, easing could be counterproductive for the labor market by raising inflation expectations and long-term interest rates, thus slowing the economy and hurting employment,” he added.

Provided there is no further evidence of above-target inflation or rising inflation expectations, Musalem said he would back more rate cuts if weaknesses in the labor market deepen. 

"I could also support lowering the nominal policy rate if expected inflation declines sustainably to 2% or below," he said. The St. Louis Fed chief noted that the inflation impact from tariffs is expected to fade later in 2026.

data.title: SB Financial signals 12.8% annual loan growth and targets $2B balance sheet amid margin and deposit focus for 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SB Financial Group (SBFG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Mark Klein, Chairman, President & CEO, highlighted that "the fourth quarter and full year 2025 results reflected continued strong execution across our franchise, delivering one of the strongest earnings quarters and year in our history." Klein emphasized disciplined lending, balanced loan and deposit growth, expense management, and maintaining strong credit fundamentals despite industry-wide mortgage pressure. Net income for the quarter was $3.9 million with diluted EPS of $0.63, up $0.08 from the prior year quarter. GAAP EPS for the year reached $2.19, a 27% lift over 2024, and tangible book value per share ended the quarter at $18, up 12.5% year-over-year. Net interest income rose to $12.7 million, an increase of nearly 17% from the previous year. Loan growth for the quarter was $70 million, or 25% annualized, and deposits increased by $45 million, or 14% annualized. The Marblehead acquisition was fully integrated, and total assets under care reached $3.6 billion. Klein stated, "Our strategy remains anchored in our key 5 strategic initiatives: Growing and diversifying revenue, greater footprint and scale for efficiency, a larger share of the client's wallet, operational excellence and, of course, always asset quality."

  • Anthony Cosentino, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "In the fourth quarter, total operating revenue increased to $16.4 million, representing a 6.3% increase from the prior year period and a 1% decrease from the linked quarter." Cosentino noted that net interest income was the primary revenue driver, up 17% year-over-year, and loan-related interest income totaled $17.3 million for the quarter. He added, "Our full year ROA was 93 basis points, up 11% from the prior year... our average rate on interest-bearing liabilities was 2.34% for the quarter, declining by 1 basis point from the prior year."

Outlook

  • Management expects to focus on disciplined execution to optimize lending capacity and drive core deposit growth in 2026. Klein explained, "We continue to see a healthy loan pipeline and benefits from a stable core deposit base that together provides a solid foundation to support performance improvement." Cosentino projected that net interest margin (NIM) will "gradually move down in 2026, probably 5 to 7 basis points... based exclusively on, I think, a higher funding cost mix."
  • The company intends to maintain positive operating leverage and targets net income growth, with a stated goal to build a $2 billion balance sheet. Klein announced a dividend of $0.155 per share, yielding approximately 2.8%, marking the 13th consecutive annual increase.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $3.9 million, and diluted EPS was $0.63. Net interest income increased to $12.7 million, with total operating revenue at $16.4 million. Loan growth reached $70 million for the quarter, and deposits rose by $45 million. Tangible book value per share increased to $18. Operating expenses declined by 2.3% from the linked quarter. Noninterest income for the year was $17.1 million. Asset quality remained strong, with nonperforming loans to total loans at 0.39% and allowance for credit losses at 1.36% of total loans.

Q&A

  • Brian Martin, Janney Montgomery Scott LLC, Research Division: Asked about margin outlook, deposit pricing pressure, and loan repricing tailwinds. Cosentino responded, "We ended the quarter with NIM at, call it, 3.51%. We're forecasting that, that's going to kind of gradually move down in 2026, probably 5 to 7 basis points... based exclusively on... a higher funding cost mix." Mark Klein added that new loans are priced "at or above the margin," and focus will be on deposits accompanying lending relationships. Steven Walz, Chief Lending Officer, noted the loan pipeline remains stable.
  • Martin: Inquired about mortgage production outlook. Cosentino projected $310 million to $325 million in production for 2026, with potential to reach $350 million to $375 million if additional lenders are added. Klein confirmed the back office can handle $400 million plus.
  • Martin: Questioned expense growth expectations. Cosentino estimated expense growth would be "in that 3.5% to 4% range," with positive operating leverage of "1.5 to 2x in 2026."
  • Martin: Asked about credit quality. Walz said improvement has been "slower than we desired" but expects continued improvement in 2026. Cosentino anticipated a reserve ratio "trends down... 3 or 4 basis points," maintaining a strong coverage position.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly positive, probing on NIM, loan growth, expense control, and credit quality, with focus on sustainability and growth expectations.
  • Management continued a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing disciplined growth and expense management. Confidence was supported by comments such as, "We remain encouraged by our positioning as we enter 2026."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remained confident, with ongoing focus on growth, margin management, and credit quality. Both management and analysts showed steady optimism, with some caution regarding rising funding costs and deposit competition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from projecting "high single-digit level of loan growth" in Q3 to reporting 12.8% annual loan growth in Q4. Management reiterated a focus on disciplined growth and operating leverage. Strategic priorities remained consistent, but Q4 saw more emphasis on deposit gathering and margin management. Analysts' questions continued to center around loan growth, margin outlook, expense control, and credit quality. Management's confidence continued, but with increased discussion of competitive deposit pricing and funding cost pressures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged competitive deposit pricing as a challenge, with Cosentino noting "deposit pricing show some stress in terms of higher competitive pricing and more requests from our current client base." There is also an expectation of "a little bit of pressure" as some deposits may move out in 2026. Margin compression and slower improvement in credit quality were cited as ongoing risks. Management plans to address these by focusing on cross-selling, targeted lender additions, and robust loan review processes.

Final Takeaway

SB Financial Group delivered robust earnings and balance sheet growth in 2025, with management emphasizing a disciplined approach to lending, deposit gathering, and expense control. As the company enters 2026, it targets continued organic loan and deposit growth, further optimization of operating leverage, and prudent credit and capital management, all while maintaining a focus on building a high-performing $2 billion balance sheet.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dayforce ticks higher on report Thoma Bravo deal to be completed in coming weeks
data.description:
Dayforce (DAY) edged higher by 0.2% after a report that its planned sale to Thoma Bravo is expected to completed by mid-February.

The US Office of the Comptroller of the Currency is expected to clear the transaction around Feb. 6, according to a CTFN report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. If OCC approval is granted in early February, the deal could be completed by mid February. 

Goldman Sachs, which is serving as a financial advisor on the deal alongside JPMorgan, is expected to fund the loan for the transaction within the next couple of weeks, according to the report.

Dayforce announced in August a definitive agreement with Thoma Bravo to become a privately held company. Under the terms of the agreement, stockholders are set to receive $70.00 per share in cash.

data.title: American Express expects 9%–10% revenue growth and EPS of $17.30–$17.90 for 2026 while expanding premium product investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Express (AXP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Squeri emphasized the company’s ongoing momentum since its 2022 long-term growth aspirations, highlighting that “full year revenues were up 10% to a record $72 billion, and EPS was $15.38, up 15% over last year, excluding the Accertify gain.” He spotlighted continued double-digit card fee growth for 30 straight quarters and strong credit quality, noting that the company remains focused on substantial investments in product refreshes, global partnerships, technology, and member experiences.
  • Squeri announced guidance for 2026, stating, “we expect 2026 revenue growth of 9% to 10% and EPS of $17.30 to $17.90.” He also disclosed a planned 16% increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.95.
  • Squeri described a strategic shift of marketing resources, saying, “we redirected marketing investments to our U.S. Platinum products given the strength we saw towards the end of the year,” and pointed to the new Platinum Card outperforming expectations in demand, engagement, and retention as the new fee is implemented.
  • CFO Christophe Le Caillec reported, “In 2025, we generated 10% revenue growth and EPS of $15.38, up 15% ex Accertify.” He underscored an 18% growth in net card fees to a record $10 billion, strong premium product demand, and high credit standards, with “delinquency rates...flat throughout the year and write-off rates remain best-in-class.”

Outlook

  • Squeri provided 2026 guidance with “revenue growth of 9% to 10% and EPS of $17.30 to $17.90.”
  • Le Caillec noted expectations for card fee growth to “pick up as the year progresses as we see the impact from the Platinum refresh, exiting the year in the high teens.” He said, “we expect credit metrics to remain generally stable with some seasonal variation in provision across quarters.”
  • Operating expenses for 2026 are projected to grow in the mid-single digits, and marketing expense is expected to be up in the low single digits.

Financial Results

  • Squeri cited “record $72 billion” in revenues and “EPS was $15.38, up 15% over last year, excluding the Accertify gain.”
  • Le Caillec reported that “net card fee grew at 18% and reached a record of $10 billion for the year,” and “total spend was up 8% FX adjusted, consistent with Q3.” International spend was up 12% FX adjusted, and retail spending increased by 10% with luxury retail up 15%.
  • Millennial and Gen Z customers now constitute the largest share of U.S. consumer spending.
  • Net interest income was up 12% for the quarter. The VCE to revenue ratio was 45% this quarter, with expectations for 44% in 2026.
  • Return on equity for the full year was 34%. The company returned $7.6 billion of capital to shareholders, including $2.3 billion of dividends and $5.3 billion of share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about shifting marketing from cashback to fee-paying cards and implications for results. Squeri responded that “we have the ability when we see opportunity to be really flexible...we saw tremendous demand for premium products,” emphasizing the focus on revenue rather than card acquisitions and increased Platinum Cards.
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: Inquired about commercial services and SME spend trends in a competitive landscape. Squeri explained that small business is strong, middle market is slower, and the market remains highly competitive, noting, “we’re still 3x larger than anybody else.”
  • Donald Fandetti, Wells Fargo: Queried about U.S. consumer billed business momentum. Squeri said, “the momentum that the new Platinum launch has given us...we’re really bullish from a consumer perspective.”
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Questioned the strategic remix toward fee-paying cards and impact on card fees. Le Caillec confirmed, “the overall portfolio is slowly getting more premium...we expect that growth rate to pick up in the balance of the year.”
  • Additional questions covered topics such as marketing spend efficiency, impact of product refreshes on engagement, competitive positioning, technology and AI investments, and regulatory risks, with management generally emphasizing strategic discipline and confidence in continued premium growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of premium card growth, cost efficiency, marketing reallocation, and competitive threats, with a tone that was generally neutral but probing, especially around cost to grow and market competition.
  • Management’s tone remained confident during prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing flexibility, strong premium demand, and a disciplined investment approach. Phrases such as “we are really happy with those decisions” and “we feel really good about '26 and beyond” were used. The tone was similar to the previous quarter, maintaining a stance of confidence and discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 remains consistent with the previous quarter’s forward-looking statements, with revenue growth targeted at 9% to 10% and mid-teens EPS growth.
  • Management continues to highlight premium product growth and disciplined expense management, with further expansion of technology and AI investments noted this quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted slightly more toward cost to grow and competitive threats in the SME and commercial sectors versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on product refresh outcomes and consumer health.
  • Management’s confidence in premium strategy and capital returns remained steady, with no notable change in sentiment or strategic direction.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified macroeconomic and political risks as primary concerns for 2026, with Squeri stating, “if you look at risk, it’s more macroeconomic or political.”
  • Competitive dynamics in both consumer and commercial sectors were acknowledged, especially with recent M&A activity among competitors and technology-driven entrants.
  • Regulatory risks, such as the proposed 10% credit card cap, were addressed with Squeri noting, “I don’t think a 10% credit card cap is the answer to that.”
  • Mitigation strategies include continued investment in technology, product innovation, and maintaining high credit standards.

Final Takeaway

American Express management conveyed confidence in delivering strong growth through 2026 with a focus on premium product strategy, disciplined capital allocation, and continued investments in technology and customer experience. The company expects record revenue and EPS, a higher dividend, and ongoing share repurchases, all underscored by robust demand for premium products, stable credit performance, and the ability to dynamically reallocate resources to the highest-return opportunities. Management remains committed to its long-term growth aspirations and believes the business is well-positioned to navigate competitive and macroeconomic challenges in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Seacoast projects 29%–31% revenue growth for 2026 following Villages acquisition and strong loan expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Seacoast Banking Corporation of Florida (SBCF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Charles Shaffer, Chairman and CEO, highlighted the closing of the Villages acquisition and strong loan growth, stating, "Loan outstandings grew at an annualized rate of 15% driven by the continued success of our commercial banking team and the additional mortgage volume contributed by the Villages acquisition." He emphasized the high quality of new residential loans and improvements in noninterest income, especially in Wealth Management, which added $550 million in new assets under management for the year.
  • Shaffer noted that overhead was well managed, with the expense ratio improving from the prior quarter, and expressed confidence in the outlook for 2026. He reported, "Our plan to drive improved shareholder returns remains firmly on track," and cited an ROA of 1.22% and return on tangible equity of 15.72% for the quarter, excluding merger-related items.
  • Tracey Dexter, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "The Seacoast team delivered a strong quarter with adjusted net income, which excludes merger-related charges increasing 18% year-over-year to $47.7 million." She reported pretax pre-provision earnings on an adjusted basis at $93.2 million for the quarter, noting a 39% increase from Q3 and a 65% increase year-over-year. Dexter mentioned, "Loan production was very strong with organic growth in balances of 15% on an annualized basis" and that deposit costs declined 14 basis points from the prior quarter to 1.67%.
  • Michael Young, Executive VP, Treasurer & Director of Investor Relations, described the strategic repositioning of the securities portfolio, stating, "Immediately following the merger close, we sold approximately $1.5 billion of the $2.5 billion securities portfolio at VBI with an emphasis on reducing risk throughout the liquidation of over $600 million in corporate debt that was sold." Young also highlighted that the portfolio yield increased 21 basis points to 4.13% in the fourth quarter.

Outlook

  • Shaffer stated, "We expect to achieve earnings per share for the full year in a range of $2.48 to $2.52 and anticipate exiting the year in the fourth quarter of 2026, after the Villages technology conversion with an ROA above 1.30% and a return on tangible equity of approximately 16%."
  • Young outlined guidance for adjusted revenue growth of 29% to 31% for the full year 2026 compared to 2025, with an expected adjusted efficiency ratio of 53% to 55%. He also indicated plans to increase banker count by approximately 15% in 2026, with the benefits fully realized in subsequent years.
  • Management expects high single-digit loan growth and low to mid-single-digit deposit growth for 2026.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net income, excluding merger-related charges, was $47.7 million for the quarter. Pretax pre-provision earnings on an adjusted basis reached $93.2 million.
  • Net interest income was $174.6 million, up 31% from the prior quarter. Net interest margin, excluding accretion on acquired loans, expanded 12 basis points to 3.44%. Loan yields increased 6 basis points to 6.02%.
  • Noninterest income was $28.6 million, a 20% increase from the prior quarter. Wealth management income grew 21% quarter-over-quarter, and total assets under management increased 37% year-over-year.
  • Noninterest expense for the quarter was $130.5 million, including $18.1 million in merger and integration costs and $23.4 million in day one credit provisions for the Villages acquisition. The adjusted efficiency ratio improved to 54.5%.
  • Deposits rose to $16.3 billion, driven by the Villages acquisition, with average balances up 29% from the prior quarter. Cost of deposits declined to 1.67%.

Q&A

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about the adjusted efficiency ratio and investments in hiring. Dexter clarified, "Our adjusted efficiency ratio includes -- leaves in the expense for amortization of intangible assets. In the past, we had excluded that."
  • Feaster followed up on the source of loan growth and the timeline for new hires' impact. Shaffer responded that roughly 10% of the 15% annualized loan growth came from legacy Seacoast, 2% to 3% from the Villages acquisition, and hiring will mostly benefit 2027 and 2028.
  • Feaster questioned capital return strategy. Shaffer explained, "We will continue to monitor... we're going to have a lot of capital that's going to give us opportunities to think about things like dividends and buybacks over time."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Inquired about margin outlook and purchase accounting. Young projected margin expansion of "probably in that 10 to 15 basis point range in the first quarter." Dexter added, "The base level of accretion is derived using the loan's contractual life... do expect volatility in that accretion number."
  • Gunther asked about further securities restructuring. Young replied, "We don't expect to execute any additional securities restructures and that's certainly not reflected in the guide."
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Asked about securities yield, to which Young replied, "Generally, it's going to be in that kind of 4.40% to 4.50% range."
  • Wood Lay, KBW: Asked about fee income and mortgage portfolio retention. Young said, "We did retain... more of the mortgage production in the fourth quarter," impacting fee income.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally positive, with questions focused on growth sustainability, efficiency, and capital deployment. There was some skepticism regarding efficiency guidance and hiring, but no overtly negative sentiment.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone both in prepared remarks and responses, repeatedly expressing confidence in the growth outlook and successful integration of acquisitions. Shaffer frequently used phrases like "I remain highly confident in our outlook for 2026."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to greater confidence, particularly regarding acquisition integration and capital deployment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from high single-digit growth to explicit projections for EPS, ROA, and revenue growth in 2026.
  • Strategic focus moved from integrating recent acquisitions to leveraging the Villages acquisition for growth and capital optimization.
  • Analysts' questions remained focused on growth, hiring, and capital, but with more emphasis on the impact of acquisitions and efficiency in this quarter.
  • Key metric differences include a significant increase in net interest income, net interest margin, and noninterest income compared to Q3, as well as higher efficiency ratio due to merger expenses.
  • Management's confidence increased, supported by improved capital and earnings projections and successful integration progress.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential volatility in purchase accounting accretion due to the Villages loan portfolio and noted that acquisition-related expenses temporarily elevate the efficiency ratio.
  • Shaffer highlighted a disciplined approach to credit risk and loan growth, stating, "We're always going to be thoughtful about what our credit risk appetite is and what we're willing to take into the portfolio."
  • Analysts raised questions on residential housing market weakness in Florida, with Shaffer describing the impact as "very market specific" and overall not as weak as advertised, though certain pockets showed overdevelopment.

Final Takeaway

Seacoast Banking Corporation closed a transformational year with robust loan and deposit growth, the successful integration of significant acquisitions, and an improved operating and capital position. Management projects meaningful adjusted revenue growth of 29% to 31% in 2026, supported by strategic investments in talent and technology, enhanced profitability, and ongoing discipline in credit and expense management. The company enters its centennial year with confidence in its ability to deliver on its ambitious financial targets and long-term shareholder value.

data.title: Colgate-Palmolive outlines 1% to 4% organic sales growth target for 2026 amid volatile category trends
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chairman, CEO & President Noel Wallace highlighted "stronger-than-expected Q4 results, and more importantly, our outlook for 2026, which marks the beginning of our new 2030 strategy." Wallace noted, "We delivered organic sales, net sales, gross profit, base business earnings per share and free cash flow growth in 2025 despite lower-than-expected category growth, higher-than-anticipated raw material inflation and the impact of higher tariffs."
  • Wallace emphasized the transition to the 2030 strategy, focusing on five areas: leveraging strong global brands, accelerating science-based innovation, expanding omnichannel demand generation, doubling down on digital, data, analytics and AI, and optimizing supply chain through predictive analytics and automation. He added, "We also announced our strategic growth and productivity program which should unlock the organizational changes and funding necessary to help us deliver on our new strategy."
  • Wallace stated, "We delivered improved momentum on our business in Q4 in terms of organic sales growth and market share, and we have seen stabilization of category growth rates as we exit 2025, but we still face significant uncertainty."
  • CFO Stanley Sutula commented, "We saw in Q4 that FX was slightly favorable versus our expectations... we see it as a low single-digit benefit to revenue in 2026, focused on primarily the first half of the year. On the bottom line, we use that as part of our flexibility in our business model, using it to invest back into the business as well as contribute to the bottom line."

Outlook

  • Wallace provided guidance for 2026, stating, "Because of this uncertainty, we're giving a wider range than normal in our net sales and organic sales growth guidance to incorporate various levels of category growth."
  • Wallace explained, "If categories get worse, we're at the low end of that guidance range, if categories stay where they are, that we're in the middle of that 1% to 4% range, more than likely and if category strengthen, we hope to obviously achieve more towards the higher end of that range."
  • Sutula added, "We see [FX] as a low single-digit benefit to revenue in 2026, focused on primarily the first half of the year."

Financial Results

  • Wallace reported "organic sales growth in all 4 categories in the fourth quarter and sequential improvement in organic sales growth versus the third quarter in every division, except North America."
  • Wallace said, "We delivered modest volume growth in Q4, excluding the impact of both the Prime100 acquisition and the planned exit of the private label business."
  • Sutula stated, "We delivered record operating cash flow of $4.2 billion. Free cash flow is also up. That came both from generating cash profit, but also really good performance on net working capital."

Q&A

  • Dara Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley: Questioned confidence in landing within the organic sales growth range and Colgate’s market share position. Wallace responded, "On an underlying basis, excluding private label, organic in excess of 3%. So a good number that we think sets us up well, but the environment continues to be very challenging and very volatile."
  • Peter Grom, UBS: Asked about Hill’s performance and category outlook. Wallace noted, "Private label was 360 basis points negative impact to volume. And despite that, if you take that out, obviously, on an underlying basis, we grew volume 2%, which is terrific."
  • Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about ad spend flexibility and rationale. Wallace stated, "While the advertising on the percent of sales was down a little bit in the fourth quarter, it was still up 5% on a dollar basis year-on-year, which is good in terms of the number of impressions and the impact we're getting in the market."
  • Peter Galbo, BofA: Probed North America strategy. Wallace acknowledged, "Tough quarter for North America, but certainly an improvement of where we saw things in the third quarter... We're obviously really gearing up with a much stronger innovation pipeline as we move into 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about category growth volatility, North America’s lagging performance, and the impact of private label and inventory shifts. The tone was slightly negative, with multiple questions seeking clarity on resilience and strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing flexibility and a robust innovation pipeline. Wallace used phrases such as, "I believe we have the correct long-term strategy, very well-designed 2026 plans," and "We are very pleased with the continued progress."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone showed increased optimism regarding momentum exiting the year, while analysts remained focused on structural challenges and sought specifics on execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a wider 1% to 4% organic sales growth guidance for 2026 due to persistent uncertainty, compared to the previous quarter’s reiteration of in-line full year organic growth.
  • Strategic focus shifted more explicitly to the 2030 strategy with new investments in digital, analytics, and AI, as well as a newly announced strategic growth and productivity program.
  • Management highlighted improved momentum in Q4, especially outside North America, and reported record operating cash flow, compared to the prior quarter’s focus on coping with volatility and sluggish category growth.
  • Analyst questions this quarter centered on guidance range, North America performance, Hill’s volume, and emerging markets, mirroring prior concerns but with increased attention to execution and flexibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Wallace cautioned that "growth rates remain low" and that the environment is "very challenging and very volatile."
  • He highlighted risks from "higher-than-anticipated raw material inflation and the impact of higher tariffs," as well as ongoing geopolitical and currency volatility.
  • The company is bracing for "higher levels of promotion and other competitive activity" and continues to watch the U.S. market for signs of improvement.
  • Sutula stated that FX benefits are being managed with flexibility to help offset volatility.

Final Takeaway

Colgate-Palmolive’s leadership emphasized that the company is entering 2026 with improved momentum and a flexible operating model amid persistent uncertainty in global consumer categories. With a new 2030 strategy, a broad innovation pipeline, and targeted investments in digital and supply chain capabilities, management believes the company is well prepared to navigate volatility, drive growth in key markets, and deliver value to shareholders through the targeted 1% to 4% organic sales growth range for the coming year.
